network.overall_strategy: This network targets L-proline by first generating racemic proline through multiple literature-grounded prebiotic routes, then channeling that racemate into a prebiotically plausible but explicitly unresolved enantiomeric enrichment stage. The strongest experimentally supported synthesis routes included are: (i) the Patel et al. cyanosulfidic pathway through a 4-carbon cyanohydrin, cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide, gamma-butyrothiolactam, and proline aminonitrile; (ii) the alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate to glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde to Delta1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate route; (iii) direct spark-discharge production of proline; and (iv) HCN polymerization/hydrolysis routes. The graph uses HCN, alpha-ketoglutarate, glutamate, Delta1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate, and racemic proline as hubs. Because the literature report makes clear that no demonstrated aqueous prebiotic route uniquely yields enantiopure L-proline, the final L-selection step is marked as a constrained speculative enrichment node based on crystal-solution eutectic amplification analogies and unresolved symmetry-breaking mechanisms. Thus the network is faithful to the literature while still delivering a connected synthesis graph to the stated target.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic formation of alpha-ketoglutarate from CO2/H2 network

conditions.pressure: 1-10 bar H2 to hydrothermal elevated pressure
conditions.catalyst: pyrite (FeS2), greigite (Fe3S4), Ni-bearing hydrothermal sulfide context
conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions pH 9-11; reductive vent chemistry; network-level protometabolic carbon fixation

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated CO2 reduction and protometabolic carboxylation/condensation chemistry within iron-sulfur hydrothermal systems generates TCA-like alpha-keto acid intermediates including alpha-ketoglutarate.
reactions.reasoning: Alpha-ketoglutarate is identified in pyrite-driven and protometabolic TCA-like networks and in nickel-catalyzed prebiotic Krebs analog chemistry. The exact single-step conversion from CO2 and H2 is a network abstraction needed to connect reported vent protometabolism to the documented alpha-ketoglutarate branch; this is therefore a compressed but literature-anchored hydrothermal entry rather than a claim of one-step synthesis. Supported by Russell & Hall (1997, 2006), Muchowska et al. (2017), Stubbs et al. (2020), and Kaur et al. (2024).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate

conditions.pressure: 1-10 bar H2 to hydrothermal elevated pressure
conditions.catalyst: FeS/FeS2 and Ni-bearing sulfide analog systems
conditions.other: anoxic; pH about 8 in Ni-catalyzed analogs, alkaline vent compatible in FeS systems; NH3 present

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid surface binding followed by reductive amination with ammonia on metal sulfide surfaces gives the corresponding amino acid glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids on sulfide minerals is directly supported by Huber & Wachtershauser (1998, 2003), and glutamate production in a prebiotic Krebs analog over nickel catalysts under mild H2/NH3 conditions is shown by Kaur et al. (2024). This is one of the strongest hydrothermal steps in the network.

reactions.name: Selective partial reduction of glutamate to glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde

conditions.other: alkaline vent to subseafloor reducing conditions; selective gamma-carboxyl reduction is the bottleneck

reactions.mechanism: Partial reduction of the side-chain carboxyl group of glutamate affords the corresponding semialdehyde without full over-reduction.
reactions.reasoning: The literature explicitly identifies glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde as the required intermediate en route to proline and notes Fe2+/FeS/H2S or H2 from serpentinization as plausible reductants, but also emphasizes that this selective reduction remains chemically difficult and experimentally unresolved under prebiotic conditions. This reaction is therefore necessary, literature-consistent, and explicitly bottlenecked. Supported conceptually by Vogel & Davis (1952), Strecker (1957), Huber & Wachtershauser (1998), and the review conclusions in the supplied report.

reactions.name: Spontaneous cyclization of glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde to Delta1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate

conditions.other: non-enzymatic intramolecular imine formation; spontaneous ring closure

reactions.mechanism: The amino aldehyde undergoes intramolecular Schiff-base formation and ring closure to produce the pyrroline carboxylate skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: This is the strongest single step in the glutamate branch: GSA cyclizes spontaneously and non-enzymatically in aqueous solution. The literature identifies this as highly prebiotically plausible and crucial for establishing the pyrrolidine skeleton. Supported directly by Vogel & Davis (1952) and Strecker (1957).

reactions.name: Reduction of Delta1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate to racemic proline

conditions.catalyst: Fe2+/FeS/H2S and H2-rich hydrothermal reducing system

reactions.mechanism: Reduction of the cyclic imine/dihydropyrroline intermediate yields proline.
reactions.reasoning: The final reduction from P5C to proline is proposed in the literature to be driven by Fe2+, FeS/H2S systems, or molecular hydrogen from serpentinization. This hydrothermal reduction is therefore a literature-grounded completion of the glutamate route, though not yet demonstrated in one continuous prebiotic system. Supported by the supplied review, Kelley et al. (2001), and hydrothermal iron-sulfur analog reasoning from Huber & Wachtershauser (1998, 2003).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic generation of 4-carbon cyanohydrin precursor

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling in phosphate-buffered anoxic solution
conditions.other: neutral pH, UV irradiation, anoxic shallow pool

reactions.mechanism: Cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry converts simple C/N feedstocks into reduced nitrile-bearing C4 intermediates that enter amino acid precursor chemistry as 4-carbon cyanohydrins.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) reported the cyanosulfidic network under 35C, neutral pH, anoxic UV-exposed conditions with HCN, H2S, phosphate, and Cu photoredox cycling, and specifically identified a 4-carbon cyanohydrin as the entry to proline movement c. This node preserves that literature-defined intermediate class.

reactions.name: Formation of cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide from 4-carbon cyanohydrin

conditions.other: neutral pH, anoxic, UV-associated sulfur chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Sulfur incorporation into the cyanohydrin-derived precursor yields open-chain and cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide species; the cyclic branch is tracked here because it is the direct proline-forming channel.
reactions.reasoning: The literature explicitly states that in Patel et al. (2015) movement c, a 4-carbon cyanohydrin reacts with H2S to give both open-chain and cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamides. The cyclic species is the crucial ring-biased precursor on the route to proline. Supported by Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: UV-driven conversion of cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide to gamma-butyrothiolactam

conditions.other: neutral pH, anoxic, shallow UV-exposed pool

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical cyclization and deoxygenation of the cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide produces the thiolactam-stage heterocycle that prefigures the pyrrolidine ring.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature identifies UV irradiation as driving cyclization and deoxygenation to gamma-butyrothiolactam in the Patel-Sutherland proline route. TiO2 is included as a plausible early Earth UV photocatalytic mineral surface in the required mineral-agent framework, while the chemistry itself is grounded in Patel et al. (2015); TiO2 photochemical relevance is additionally supported by Saladino et al. (2017).

reactions.name: Photoreduction and cyanide addition to gamma-butyrothiolactam yielding proline aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: The thiolactam undergoes photoreduction and HCN addition to furnish the alpha-aminonitrile immediate precursor to proline.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) specifically reported that gamma-butyrothiolactam undergoes photoreduction and HCN addition to generate the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of proline (compound 47). This is the most direct literature-supported route to a discrete proline precursor.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of proline aminonitrile to racemic proline
reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis through amide/acids gives the amino acid proline.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied review states that hydrolysis of the proline aminonitrile yields proline itself in the Patel et al. route. This is standard aminonitrile-to-amino-acid chemistry and is directly tied to Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to diaminomaleonitrile
reactions.mechanism: HCN tetramerization produces DAMN as a canonical oligomerization intermediate within HCN polymer chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the established HCN tetramer and a key node in HCN oligomer chemistry. The literature review cites Oró (1961) and Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) for HCN polymerization pathways leading, after further chemistry and hydrolysis, to amino acids including proline.

reactions.name: Hydrolytic release of racemic proline from HCN polymer/DAMN-derived chemistry

conditions.temperature: elevated surface heating to hydrolysis conditions
conditions.other: higher-temperature polymer hydrolysis favored according to reviewed experiments

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomers evolve through polymeric and likely hydantoin-like intermediates; hydrolysis liberates amino acids including proline as part of a complex mixture.
reactions.reasoning: The review notes that HCN oligomerization produces amino acids through polymer hydrolysis, with proline detected under some conditions, especially higher temperature experiments, and that the route proceeds through DAMN and hydantoin intermediates. Because the detailed downstream polymer chemistry is heterogeneous, this reaction is intentionally represented as a literature-backed compressed release step rather than a false one-step mechanism. Supported by Oró (1961) and Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge atmospheric synthesis of HCN

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge, variable local high energy
conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge in reduced atmosphere; mineral deposition sinks included
conditions.other: CH4/N2/H2O with trace NH3 in classic apparatus; atmospheric lightning simulation

reactions.mechanism: Electric discharge in reduced atmospheres forms nitriles including HCN, which then dissolve into aqueous reservoirs and feed downstream surface chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The review emphasizes Miller-Urey-type spark discharge chemistry as a major abiotic source of amino acids and related organics; HCN is a canonical intermediate in such atmospheric discharge networks. Using the correct feedstocks CH4, N2, and H2O addresses earlier oversimplification. Supported by Ring et al. (1972), Johnson et al. (2008), and Jiang et al. (2014).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge direct formation of racemic proline

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge, variable local high energy
conditions.other: reduced volcanic or methane-rich atmosphere; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Complex plasma-driven radical and nitrile chemistry in spark discharge systems yields amino acid mixtures containing proline directly.
reactions.reasoning: Direct experimental detection of proline in spark discharge experiments is strongly supported by Ring et al. (1972), Johnson et al. (2008), and Jiang et al. (2014). The literature also warns that proline is often undercounted because OPA/NAC derivatization misses secondary amines, as noted by Parker et al. (2011), reinforcing that this direct route is likely more significant than commonly recognized.

reactions.name: Biochemical-style transamination entry from ornithine to glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde

conditions.other: included as literature-noted alternative entry point but experimentally unexplored prebiotically

reactions.mechanism: Ornithine can, in biology, feed glutamic-gamma-semialdehyde/P5C chemistry through aminotransfer-like conversion.
reactions.reasoning: The review explicitly identifies ornithine as an alternative entry point in biology but states that comparable non-enzymatic prebiotic transformations remain unexplored. This reaction is therefore included only as a low-confidence connector to preserve a literature-mentioned branch and is explicitly speculative. Supported as a biological analogy and open question by the supplied review and Strecker (1957).

reactions.name: Enantiomeric enrichment of racemic proline to L-proline

conditions.other: crystal-solution eutectic enrichment analog; not demonstrated under fully prebiotic aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: Partial deracemization/enrichment is proposed via differential solubility of racemic versus enantiopure crystalline proline phases during repeated dissolution and reprecipitation.
reactions.reasoning: The literature is explicit that no robust prebiotic aqueous route to enantiopure L-proline has been demonstrated. However, Hayashi et al. (2006) and Klussmann et al. (2006) showed strong crystal-solution eutectic amplification for proline in non-prebiotic solvents, making enrichment from low ee to high ee chemically real even if not yet geochemically validated. This reaction is therefore necessary to meet the requested target definition but is intentionally flagged as unresolved/speculative in prebiotic relevance, consistent with the review's conclusion.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal alpha-ketoglutarate to proline core route
pathways.description: Protometabolic hydrothermal chemistry yields alpha-ketoglutarate, which is reductively aminated to glutamate, partially reduced to GSA, cyclized to P5C, and reduced to racemic proline before enrichment to L-proline.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic Patel-Sutherland route
pathways.description: HCN and H2S in UV-irradiated neutral anoxic surface pools generate a 4-carbon cyanohydrin, cyclic alpha-hydroxythioamide, gamma-butyrothiolactam, and proline aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to proline and then undergoes enrichment.

pathways.name: Spark-derived HCN feeding cyanosulfidic proline synthesis
pathways.description: Atmospheric spark discharge generates HCN, which is transferred into surface cyanosulfidic pools and routed through the Patel-Sutherland sequence to proline.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge direct proline route
pathways.description: Direct atmospheric discharge chemistry yields racemic proline as a minor but reproducible amino acid product, followed by unresolved enantiomeric enrichment.

pathways.name: HCN oligomerization and polymer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: HCN oligomerizes to DAMN and downstream polymeric intermediates that release racemic proline upon hydrolysis; L-proline requires a later enrichment step.

pathways.name: Spark-to-HCN-polymer route
pathways.description: Spark discharge supplies HCN that then enters oligomerization/polymer hydrolysis chemistry to give racemic proline.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal to surface cross-over route via proline pool convergence
pathways.description: Hydrothermal glutamate/P5C chemistry yields racemic proline, which converges with surface-derived proline pools before enrichment, highlighting environmental interconnection.

pathways.name: HCN-to-cyanosulfidic-to-proline aminonitrile route
pathways.description: Independent of spark origin, environmental HCN acts as a hub that can be funneled into the cyclic thioamide and proline aminonitrile branch, then hydrolyzed to proline.

pathways.name: Ornithine alternative entry into the GSA/P5C branch
pathways.description: A speculative but literature-mentioned alternative entry starts from ornithine, feeding GSA and then the spontaneous P5C cyclization and reduction steps to proline.

pathways.name: Convergent multi-source proline pool to L-proline target
pathways.description: Racemic proline formed independently by spark discharge, HCN polymer hydrolysis, cyanosulfidic aminonitrile hydrolysis, and hydrothermal P5C reduction converges into a common proline pool that is then subjected to enrichment.
